a.headline: 'Who is going to pay us when we're replaced by robots?' The Indian factory workers told to film themselves for AI
a.summary: Factory workers in India were asked to wear cameras and document their own movements to train AI systems that could eventually replace them — raising urgent questions about labour, consent, and who bears the cost of automation. This piece is a stark reminder that AI's disruption of work is already happening at the most vulnerable end of global supply chains.

a.headline: You're only supposed to blow the bloody hooves off: AI Michael Caine narrates Odyssey audiobook
a.summary: ElevenLabs has released an officially licensed AI replica of Michael Caine's voice to narrate a retelling of Homer's Odyssey, marking a significant moment in the commercialisation of voice likeness. This raises immediate questions for designers, animators, and creative professionals about the future value of human-performed creative labour.

a.headline: US AI stock sell-off shakes markets from Wall Street to Asia
a.summary: A major sell-off in AI-related stocks spread from Wall Street to Asian markets as investors began questioning the enormous valuations and infrastructure spending underpinning the AI boom. For design students tracking the industry, this signals growing financial uncertainty around the very companies reshaping creative tool ecosystems.

a.headline: Readers say "there's no such thing as an AI artist" in this week's Dezeen Debate
a.summary: Dezeen's reader debate erupted after featuring AI-generated renders of a speculative Gaudí hotel, with many commenters flatly rejecting the label 'AI artist' as a legitimate creative identity. The discussion cuts to the heart of ongoing tensions between computational image-making and the cultural authority traditionally granted to human authorship.

b.headline: Majority of datacenters are vulnerable to climate threats like floods and fires, study finds
b.summary: A new study warns that the AI datacentres driving the current tech boom are highly exposed to the very climate hazards — floods, wildfires, extreme heat — that their own greenhouse gas emissions are accelerating. This feedback loop between AI infrastructure and climate breakdown is essential context for any designer thinking critically about the environmental cost of digital tools.

b.headline: Chinese supercomputer leapfrogs best US machines to be ranked world's fastest
b.summary: China's LineShine supercomputer has topped the global Top500 rankings, intensifying the geopolitical race to build ever more powerful — and energy-hungry — computing infrastructure. The escalating scale of these machines directly feeds into the environmental cost of AI training and inference at a national level.

c.headline: Animator Dhrutika Khimani rejects digital tools and favours the tactility of hands on experimentation
c.summary: Stop-motion animator Dhrutika Khimani works deliberately with threads, paper, and physical materials, choosing tactile experimentation over digital shortcuts to tell deeply personal stories. Her practice is a quiet but pointed counter-position to the acceleration of AI-assisted animation, centring slow craft and embodied making.

c.headline: Australia 'sleepwalking' into AI crisis and 'tech bro free-for-all', says Greens senator
c.summary: Australian politicians are calling for urgent regulation to prevent tech companies from training AI models on Australian creative content without consent or compensation. This political pushback reflects a broader global movement of creators and legislators pushing back against the unchecked extraction of creative work by AI firms.

c.headline: 'Navigating the unknown together': me and my idiot AI boyfriend
c.summary: A writer who is deeply sceptical of AI reluctantly tests an AI companion chatbot, producing a critical and personal essay about seduction, absurdity, and the social engineering baked into these products. It's a useful piece of critical writing for students examining how AI systems are designed to manufacture emotional attachment.

c.headline: De'Longhi's Tiny Coffee Shop film is a stop-motion marvel
c.summary: Model maker Simon Weisse and director Sam Walker built a meticulously handcrafted miniature street scene for De'Longhi, deliberately choosing physical stop-motion over CGI or AI-generated imagery. The work stands as an implicit argument for the irreplaceable value of human skill, time, and material intelligence in commercial image-making.
